Takeaway: Encode named PM frameworks as individually triggerable skills where the frontmatter description carries BOTH provenance and a 'Use when…' trigger — this makes auto-invocation match the PM's intent and gives every skill an auditable lineage (great for an enablement catalog where 'which method, by whom' matters).

Adopt the noun/verb split: a library of auto-loaded knowledge skills + a thin layer of user-invoked /commands that chain them, with commands scoped to reference only skills in the same pack. It keeps the catalog composable and prevents tangled cross-dependencies.

Ship a validate_plugins.py-style CI gate from day one: enforce name=directory, required frontmatter fields, semver, trigger-phrase presence, body word-count bounds, same-plugin cross-refs, and README sections — turns 'skill quality' from taste into a passing check, which is essential when many PMs contribute skills.

Use the document-intent-first pattern for any review/audit workflow: generate the spec/doc set as an explicit artifact, then run audits AGAINST that artifact rather than the raw code — and bake 'self-refute every finding, report only evidence-backed risks' into the audit prompt to control hallucination without needing a separate critic agent.

Make portability a first-class layer: a single universal SKILL.md format plus one marketplace manifest that installs on Claude Code/Cowork and degrades gracefully on Codex (plain-language invocation) and copy-to-folder on Cursor/Gemini — so an enablement program isn't locked to one harness.

Note the deliberate limitation: this repo is fully stateless (no memory, no subagents, no persisted artifacts). For a corporate AI-PM OS you'd layer a context/memory tier on top — the skill catalog itself is harness- and memory-agnostic, which is a feature for reuse.

Adopt a typed skill taxonomy (component / interactive / workflow) with `type` in frontmatter as the orchestration axis — it makes 'which skill, and does it ask questions or just produce an artifact?' explicit and lets workflow skills compose the smaller ones.

Standardize an 'Adaptive Decision Ladder' shape for every advisory/diagnosis skill: 3-5 named-dimension questions, 3-4 numbered options each with an inline consequence gloss + a custom escape hatch, then a recommendation with rationale, an alternative-with-tradeoffs, and reassessment triggers. Highly transferable to corporate AI-PM enablement (prioritization, build-vs-buy, framework selection).

Factor the interaction UX into ONE shared 'facilitation' skill (entry modes, one-question-per-turn, progress labels like 'Q3/8', multi-select parsing, 'recommendations only at decision points', closing 'Assumptions to Validate') and have all interactive skills reference it — keeps a large skill library consistent and short, and gives a clean conflict-precedence rule.

Make slash-commands self-documenting workflow routers: declare a `uses:` skill list and `outputs:` deliverables in command frontmatter, number the workflow steps with backticked skill names, add checkpoint gates and 'Next Steps' routing — non-technical PMs run /discover instead of stitching skills by hand.

Encode the house writing/output standard in a root CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md (fixed skill anatomy, a Quality Checklist, Tone Do/Don't, and a load-bearing 'don't strip the teaching' rule) so every generated skill is consistent and pedagogic — and ship the same markdown skills cross-harness (Claude Code plugin + Codex zip + Streamlit playground) so the enablement program isn't locked to one tool.
Generate the skill catalog/index from frontmatter via a script (+ CI) so the 'map of skills' never drifts from reality — a cheap, vector-DB-free discoverability layer for a growing library.

Ship every internal PM skill as a triad — method (SKILL.md) + fill-in structure (TEMPLATE.md) + a worked, company-specific EXAMPLE.md — so enablement output calibrates to your org's good artifacts, not generic AI prose.

Make auto-invocation a tested contract: give each skill a small labeled eval set (trigger / no-trigger + near-miss negatives against confusable sibling skills) and run router evals in CI so 'the right skill fires' is measured, not hoped for.

Encode refusal and anti-fabrication rules inside the skill itself (no invented baselines/targets, label assumptions/placeholders, require guardrail counter-metrics) so quality and honesty travel with the skill rather than depending on reviewer vigilance.

Use a separate read-only critic agent that reviews each artifact against its originating skill's own contract and grades P0–P3 with a mandatory concrete fix per finding — one source of truth for both authoring and judging.

Drive workflow governance off a PRODUCED/EMPTY/FAILED result taxonomy with explicit go/no-go checkpoints (auto-advance only on substantive output, halt on failure) so multi-step PM workflows are safely automatable.
Prime sessions with a lightweight phase-detection hook (branch + artifact signals → top-N relevant skills injected) so PMs get the right tool surfaced without knowing the catalog.

Back the whole library with multi-platform CI validators (count parity, cross-references, placeholder/fabricated-metric/quote-sourcing scans, validator-parity) so a growing skill catalog can't silently rot.

Write trigger-rich skill descriptions, not commands: the production-grade pattern here is one SKILL.md per PM activity with a description that literally lists 'Use when...' scenarios, so the model auto-selects the skill from the user's natural phrasing — a corporate AI-PM program should invest enablement effort in description quality + the underlying methodology, not in slash-command UX.

Abstract integrations behind capability categories (~~tracker, ~~chat, ~~analytics) with one connector-resolution doc, so the same skill library ships unchanged across an org running Jira-vs-Linear or Slack-vs-Teams — critical for enterprise rollout where tool stacks vary by team.

Encode the PM craft as hard output contracts + named frameworks inside the skill: P0 discipline ('if everything is P0, nothing is P0'), RICE/ICE/MoSCoW, RAG+ROAM, thematic-analysis 5-steps, North-Star/L1/L2 metric tiers, and explicit anti-hallucination rules (attribute quotes by participant type, report confidence, use ranges) — this is what makes outputs senior-PM-grade rather than generic.

Reserve an explicit slash command only for interactive/stateful work (the brainstorming thinking-partner) and let document-generation skills self-trigger — minimizes command sprawl and matches commands to where a user genuinely needs a deliberate launch point.

Connector config as declarative .mcp.json (remote HTTP-MCP + OAuth), versioned per plugin, makes the integration surface auditable and swappable without touching skill logic — a clean template for an internal AI-PM plugin that must pass security review.

Trust-but-verify the README: the advertised '7 slash commands' did not match the code (1 command, 8 skills, including an undocumented sprint-planning skill) — for an enablement program, treat the actual SKILL.md/.mcp.json files as ground truth, not the marketing README.

Encode each PM framework as a SKILL.md with a hard Output contract (required deliverables) plus explicit When-to-Use / When-Not-to-Use gates, and enforce that structure with a linter so skills can't degrade — copy crux-diagnosis's scored-rubric + options-table shape and validate_skills.py's checks.

Ship every skill with a cross-runtime sidecar manifest (one method file, two front-doors) so the same enablement library works whether a PM is on Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor — and lint name/version/trigger consistency between them.
Make skill ROUTING itself a tested artifact: a small keyword/route-cases eval that asserts 'this prompt should trigger JTBD-extraction' catches mis-firing skills before users do.

Adopt the session-eval → instruction-file loop: a fixed eval template with axial-code checkboxes and an explicit 'what to change in AGENTS.md' field, reviewed weekly, turns every agent session into durable process improvement.

Use a thin two-stage intake→synthesis split (like ost-intake) for any heavy analysis: a normalization skill that interrogates inputs, reframes them to the right shape, and hands a clean typed artifact to the analysis skill — better inputs beat better prompts.

Constrain subagents with a 3-field output contract (assumptions / result / checks-run) and a fixed Research→Plan→Verify order, with explicit 'don't use subagents for trivial work' rules — gives a context firewall without overhead.

Make goal-citation a hard gate in the agent contract: an AI-PM task generator should refuse to create a task that doesn't cite an OKR/goal, and should cap how many top-priority items can exist — enforced relevance beats aspirational prioritization.

Ship deterministic guardrail tools (dedup + ambiguity-detection) as MCP/code rather than relying on the LLM to 'remember' to avoid duplicates; a cheap SequenceMatcher + vague-verb regex prevents the two most common backlog failure modes and forces clarifying questions.

Adopt the session-eval loop as enablement telemetry: trace participants' Claude Code sessions into graded eval cards with a small fixed taxonomy of axial codes; this gives a corporate program a concrete, reviewable way to coach 'good context gathering' and 'efficient tool use'.

Keep memory as a small, ordered, always-read file stack (instructions → goals → state → knowledge) with markdown traversal instead of a vector store — far easier for PMs to inspect, edit, and trust than opaque RAG.

Package integrations as a self-installing skill triad (README + SETUP_SKILL.md + mcp-config.json) so non-technical PMs can add tools by asking the agent, lowering the setup barrier for an enablement rollout.
Use a one-command scaffolder (setup.sh) that generates a personalized GOALS.md and the folder tree, so every participant starts from a populated, consistent context structure.

For a corporate AI-PM enablement skill, prefer ONE well-routed multi-domain skill over many fragmented skills when domains share a voice and workflow: a single intent→framework→file routing table is cheaper to maintain and lets the model lazy-load only the needed module — keeps the always-on context tiny.

Ship quality gates in two tiers: a small universal set in the root file (assumptions labeled, outcomes measurable, roles specific, tradeoffs named) plus domain-specific gates colocated inside each knowledge module so they only load with that domain. This is a directly copyable pattern for enforcing SoftServe output standards.

Adopt a STATUS completion contract (DONE / DONE_WITH_CONCERNS / BLOCKED / NEEDS_CONTEXT) for every deliverable — it makes AI-PM outputs auditable and surfaces weak work instead of papering over it; pair with a '3 attempts then escalate' rule to prevent low-quality grinding.

Separate an always-on 'framing gate' (one-turn quality-control pushback) from opt-in 'coaching' (multi-round interrogation). The framing gate prevents garbage on every request without nagging; coaching is consent-gated. This balances rigor against annoyance for a wide internal user base.

Encode coaching as Trigger/Action/Stop-When tables with explicit exit conditions (max 2 rounds, or stop when the user supplies the missing segmentation). Bounded challenge loops are what make 'be a demanding peer' usable in production rather than an infinite Socratic loop.

Use the Skill+Template+Example triad per deliverable (method file / structure scaffold / worked output with 'why this matters'), and treat templates explicitly as scaffolding-not-filler ('thinking matters more than format') to avoid placeholder-filling behavior.

Add a release validator (bin/validate-release-style) to CI for the skill repo: version sync, required files present, manifest covers assets, docs aligned across languages, and regression guards on prompt content — a cheap way to keep a growing skill pack trustworthy.

A high-value PM skill can be ~25 lines: name+description frontmatter, a 3-5 step method, and an Output section of required headings ending in a forced verdict. Lower the authoring bar this far and non-engineer PMs can write their own skills.

Steer output with an empty-heading skeleton plus a constrained verdict enum (e.g. Ready/At Risk/Not Ready) rather than prose instructions — it's self-documenting, lint-able, and produces consistent, decision-terminating outputs.
Favor decision/diagnosis skills (return a judgment) over doc-generators for an AI-PM program — they augment reasoning and are easier to evaluate against a 'did it reach a defensible verdict' bar.
Pair every skill with exactly one filled worked-example file, and make the whole skill set share a single fictional product so the example corpus doubles as an end-to-end training case study.
Put the auto-invocation trigger inside the description's 'Use when ...' clause; with no separate trigger field, a well-written description is both discovery and routing.

Organizing skills into lifecycle categories (Problem Discovery / Prioritization / Validation / Delivery & Launch) in the README — even with a flat skills/ folder — gives newcomers a mental map without needing an index file or orchestration layer.

A flat library of 12-20 named-methodology skills, each one SKILL.md of 50-150 lines, is a high-leverage starting point for an AI-PM enablement program — small enough to author and review by hand, broad enough to cover discovery->strategy->prioritization->PRD->launch.

Enforce an authoring checklist (CONTRIBUTING-style): every skill must cite a real framework, use ALWAYS/NEVER/CRITICAL rules with a concrete counter-example, ban corporate jargon, and stay 50-150 lines. This keeps a crowd-sourced skill pack consistent without needing CI.
Encode method as imperative rules + one worked counter-example, not prose — agents follow 'NEVER define jobs as features; the job is X' far more reliably than a paragraph explaining JTBD.

Bake trigger phrases into the description ('Use when asked to write a PRD, product spec, feature requirements...') so skills auto-fire on natural PM language and stay portable across harnesses (no per-tool config).

Bake output contracts into the skill itself (mandatory sections, P0/P1/P2, counter-metrics, 'show the RICE math', confidence<100% without data) so deliverables are gap-aware by construction rather than relying on a separate review agent.
Ship a one-line install path (plugin marketplace + npx CLI + submodule) and a manifest that enumerates every skill — distribution friction is what kills internal enablement libraries.
Selectively bundle fill-in templates in a references/ subfolder only where a structured artifact is the deliverable (interview guide), rather than forcing a template on every skill.

Anchor an entire skill library to ONE shared context file (company/product/market/metrics/goals) that every skill is required to read first — this is the cheapest, highest-leverage way to make generic PM skills produce org-specific output without RAG.

Encode skill relationships declaratively in frontmatter (`reads:` for context deps, `related:` for workflow chaining) so the agent can traverse a skill graph by following pointers — a map-file approach that scales to 50 skills with no retrieval infra.
Right-size artifacts with an explicit scope gate (lightweight vs full PRD by company stage) as workflow step 1 — prevents over-engineering early-stage docs.

Publish an authoring contract (fixed frontmatter fields + fixed body sections + 'must read shared context') so contributed skills stay uniform and trustworthy — treat the contract as the quality gate even before CI exists.
Bake assumption-tracking with evidence + invalidation criteria into doc-generation skills so outputs are falsifiable, not just plausible.
Ship a one-command, harness-agnostic installer (`npx skills add`) with cherry-pick and list flags so an enablement program can roll out a curated subset per role.

Teach the 'CLAUDE.md = durable identity/company-context/voice (loads every session); skills = task playbooks (load on trigger)' split as the first principle of an AI-PM enablement program — it's the cleanest token-economy mental model and the repo's whole thesis.

Ship a SKILL-TEMPLATE.md with a fixed section contract (Trigger / Context / Instructions / Constraints / Output Format / Examples / Anti-patterns) so every PM authors skills in one shape; pair it with a 5-step 'create your own skill' tutorial to make authoring self-serve.

Use contrastive Good-vs-Bad examples on the same input, annotated with the failure reason — this raises output consistency faster than adding more instructions (the repo explicitly notes 'Examples teach Claude patterns faster than instructions').
Encode PM frameworks as scoring GUIDES with concrete numeric thresholds + a final verdict label (GO/ITERATE/STOP) so judgment becomes a repeatable, auditable scorecard.
Build an interactive elicitation gate (3-4 clarifying questions, with coded pushback on vague answers) into every generation skill so the model never produces confident output on thin input.
Bake anti-hallucination markers ('[NEED: …]', 'ASSUMPTION: …') and rating caps into the output contract so missing data is surfaced rather than fabricated.

Anchor every PM agent on a small, fixed, version-controllable context folder (8 markdown files) and make 'read context first, ask only for gaps' a hard rule in each agent's prompt — this is the single biggest lever for product-specific (vs generic) AI output and the cheapest thing to replicate in an enterprise PM enablement kit.

Use the filesystem as the inter-agent bus: have discovery/research subagents WRITE provenance-stamped facts to shared context files and have downstream agents re-read them, rather than passing large blobs back through the orchestrator — keeps the main window clean and makes every artifact auditable.

Encode methodology AND its anti-patterns: skills here name the framework's author (RICE=Intercom, Devil's Advocacy) AND list failure modes (Consensus Theater, False Precision, TAM-fantasy), which trains better judgment than templates alone.

Model-tier the fleet in frontmatter: a cheap model for mechanical file-scanning, frontier models for reasoning and adversarial review — a one-line cost-control pattern worth standardizing across a corporate agent catalog.

Make high-stakes decisions go through a structured 3-phase debate (independent -> cross-examine -> weighted synthesis with documented dissent) and bake a named adversary that rates its own confidence — turns 'ask the AI' into a defensible BUILD/DON'T-BUILD verdict suitable for stakeholder review.

Auto-select the right artifact heavyweight via a transparent scored rubric (3-axis 0-10 complexity -> Lean/Comprehensive/Google PRD) and show the reasoning — gives non-PMs right-sized rigor without choosing templates themselves.
Ship as installable Claude Code plugins with a marketplace.json so an org can roll out a versioned PM toolkit (core + optional advanced team presets) and let users install only what they need.

Ship enablement as a sub-60-line fill-in CLAUDE.md form (Role/Product/Primary metric/Guardrails/OKRs/Terminology) rather than a wiki — a PM is productive in 2 minutes, and the brevity itself improves instruction-following. Pair with a hard 'prune at ~150 lines, move domain knowledge to skills' rule.

A reviewer-persona TABLE plus a 'review as [role]' verbal contract gives every PM a multi-lens critique (engineer/designer/exec/skeptic/customer/data-analyst) with zero agent tooling — the cheapest high-leverage piece to standardize across a team.
Make every skill an elicitation gate first: 3-5 mandatory clarifying questions with 'do NOT proceed until answered' kills blind generation, the #1 complaint about AI PM output.

Bake anti-hallucination into the output contract with `[NEED: data from X]` / `[NEEDS INSTRUMENTATION]` placeholders and a banned-words list — turns 'don't fabricate' from a hope into a visible, gradable artifact.

Teach via Good-vs-Bad pairs and an Anti-Patterns catalogue inside each skill, not just a target template; PMs learn the failure mode (vague metric, evidence-free problem statement) faster than the ideal.

Encode the deliverable as a staged lifecycle (PRD stages 1-6 ending in a post-launch results link) so the same doc is the durable source of truth across the build-measure-learn loop — a reusable pattern for roadmaps, launches, OKRs.

Standardize session hygiene as policy: /clear between unrelated tasks, a ~40-50 exchange cap, and a HANDOFF.md (state/decisions/open-questions/next-steps) round-trip with `claude --continue` — portable to any corporate Claude Code rollout.
Add a self-improvement protocol that routes corrections by altitude (one-off -> propose a CLAUDE.md rule with approval; recurring -> a scoped rules file) so team configs compound without bloating.

Separate orchestration from capability: build a flat library of single-purpose /slash skills (each a SKILL.md with name+description frontmatter and an enforced output contract), then add thin 'agent' files that just sequence those skills with approval gates. AI-PMs can run any skill alone or invoke a full pipeline, and you maintain logic in one place.

Make the folder taxonomy the context store: a fixed initiatives/<name>/{interviews,opportunities,assumptions,solutions,prd,tasks} tree (scaffolded by one setup command copying a _templates dir with .gitkeep) gives every skill predictable read/write slots — no vector DB needed, and onboarding is 'just open the repo'.

Encode a named methodology end-to-end, not as a topic but as a sequenced artifact chain: Continuous Discovery becomes snapshots→synthesis→opportunities→solutions→assumptions, each step a skill that consumes the prior artifact — teaches the framework by making the tool enforce it.

Bake an always-on anti-sycophancy / sparring rule into the root instruction file: ban hedging phrases, enumerate the domain's classic failure patterns to flag, and demand specificity (named users over categories). This single alwaysApply rule raises the quality of every interaction.

Use interactive elicitation gates before generation (ask 3–5 numbered+lettered clarifying questions; refuse to proceed past ideation until the human supplies their own ideas) to prevent the agent from producing confident, ungrounded PM docs.

Quantitative prioritization can be fully prompt-encoded: ICE with explicit mapping tables and evidence-type weighting + a 'score only on stated evidence' guard delivers rigor without shipping or maintaining code.

Standardize a single agent-file anatomy and enforce it across every role: frontmatter (name/description-as-trigger/tools-allowlist/persona-vibe) + body sections Identity&Memory → Mission → Critical Rules (hard gates) → Deliverable Templates → Workflow Phases → Success Metrics. New PM agents become fill-in-the-blanks, and the uniformity is what makes downstream tooling (install, lint, transpile) possible.

Bundle the OUTPUT TEMPLATES inside the agent, not just the persona. An AI-PM enablement skill should carry the actual PRD/Opportunity-Assessment/Roadmap/GTM/Sprint-Health markdown skeletons (with metric tables that force baseline/target/measurement-window and a 'what we are NOT building' section) so the model produces house-style artifacts, not generic prose.

Encode hard process gates as 'Critical Rules' the agent must obey: ≥5 problem interviews before solution talk, no roadmap item without owner+metric+horizon, PRFAQ before PRD, a blocker open >24h is a failure. These convert a methodology into checkable behavior.

For discovery, run a fan-out of role-scoped agents on the SAME brief with an explicit 'work independently, do not coordinate' instruction, then a synthesis pass that reports BOTH where they converged (signals) and where they disagreed (tensions for a human to decide)—a fast, honest alternative to a single mega-prompt.

Keep one canonical source and a converter so the same PM playbook runs in whatever tool the org uses (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex). Author once in markdown+frontmatter; let a build step inject each harness's required metadata.

Adopt a fixed memory-bank path convention (spec-as-source-of-truth file in, task/decision artifacts out, keyed by project slug) so multi-step agent work is resumable and auditable without bespoke plumbing.

A reusable house-style system-prompt skeleton (identity → numbered responsibilities → named-framework blocks → anti-patterns → output templates → metrics) lets a team author many consistent, high-quality PM agents fast — give AI-PM trainees this skeleton as a fill-in-the-blanks template.

Put the framework knowledge (RICE/Kano/JTBD/OKR) INSIDE the agent prompt as an explicit menu plus a fenced decision template — this is the cheapest way to make a generalist model behave like a disciplined PM, and it's directly liftable into a SoftServe AI-PM enablement pack.
Encode invocation as few-shot <example> blocks in the description field: teach PMs that WHEN an agent fires is itself an artifact you curate (add examples for the cases you want caught), not magic.

Per-agent least-privilege `tools:` allowlists are a simple, auditable governance lever — only the orchestrator gets `Task`; researchers get web tools; planners get TodoWrite — a clean model for role/permission boundaries in an enterprise rollout.

Numeric decision gates written in prose (trend-researcher's momentum/growth thresholds; sprint-prioritizer's P0/P1/P2 + Include/Defer/Cut) turn fuzzy PM judgment into repeatable rubrics — a pattern worth standardizing across an org's prioritization agents.

CAUTION for a corporate program: this repo's lack of any shared context file means every cadence/value is duplicated per agent and can drift — a jumpstart should ADD a CLAUDE.md/constitution layer that these agents reference rather than re-state, getting the best of both.

Install simplicity (clone → copy .md into ~/.claude/agents/ → restart) lowers adoption friction to near-zero; a corporate pack can mirror this with a one-command installer while adding the missing governance/context layer.

The cheapest way to stand up a multi-role AI-PM team is crewAI's two-file config pattern: roles in agents.yaml (role/goal/backstory), workflow in tasks.yaml (description/expected_output/context/agent) — a SoftServe jumpstart could ship a parameterizable team where trainees only edit YAML, never Python.

Model the PM artifact pipeline as a DAG via per-task `context:` lists; the final synthesis task fans in all upstream artifacts. This teaches doc-handoff thinking (each role produces the input for the next) without any orchestration code.

Inject a shared 'spec-writing constitution' (rules.txt) as crew-level knowledge so every agent obeys the same standards — a reusable governance file (design-before-code, start small, TDD, gap-flag) is more durable than baking rules into each persona prompt.

Make 'optimized for the downstream agentic coder' an explicit, repeated output requirement; framing PRDs as machine-consumed context (not human docs) is a concrete, teachable shift for AI-PM enablement.
Embed a critic role inside the sequential chain (the SME review task feeding the designer) as a low-cost quality gate, instead of relying on a separate adversarial verification pass.

Encode named PM frameworks directly into the agent persona, not just the docs: BMAD's PM bakes 'think like Cagan/Torres, write with Bezos six-pager discipline; PRDs from interviews not template-filling' into customize.toml — a corporate AI-PM program can ship role personas whose principles ARE the company's product operating model.

Make the artifact the handoff contract: a status-gated, checklist-validated story/PRD file with a mandatory 'References (source path+section)' block lets each stage hand a fresh agent exactly the cited context it needs — adopt 'every step writes the doc the next step reads, and citations are required' as the enablement standard.

Shard big docs into ##-section files + an index.md and load only the relevant shard (SELECTIVE_LOAD) — gives just-in-time retrieval over a knowledge base with zero vector infrastructure, ideal for a PM org that lives in markdown/Confluence-style docs.

Separate the persona-router (human-facing menu agent) from the worker skills it dispatches to, and force skill:name invocation (no cross-skill file paths) — keeps a growing skill library refactorable and lets non-engineers navigate via numbered menus.

Ship a base→team→user customization layer so a central skills team owns the canonical methodology while each squad/PM tunes voice, persistent project facts, and activation steps without forking — the key to scaling one skill set across many teams.

Run a deterministic skill-validator in CI (frontmatter triggers, naming, cross-ref hygiene, JSON output) before any skill merges — a corporate program needs this to keep dozens of contributed skills consistent and trustworthy.

Distribute planning skills as web bundles for ChatGPT/Gemini so non-technical PMs do discovery in cheap subscriptions, then graduate the artifacts into the engineering harness — a pragmatic cost/adoption on-ramp.

Add a fresh-context adversarial reviewer that must find ≥N issues and treats zero findings as suspicious, run against every spec/PRD before sign-off, to counter confirmation bias in agent-generated docs.

Make the spec the database: have PMs produce a frontmatter'd PRD/epic/task tree that agents read and write, and round-trip it into your existing issue tracker (Jira/GitHub) so AI work is visible in the same tooling the org already uses — no separate 'AI memory' silo.

Force an elicitation gate before any generation: a mandatory 5-question discovery (problem/users/success/out-of-scope/constraints) plus quality gates (every user story needs acceptance criteria, every success metric measurable) is a cheap, copyable anti-placeholder contract for PM artifacts.

Separate deterministic from probabilistic work: push all status/rollup/validation to plain scripts and tell the model to just run them — saves tokens, removes hallucinated status, and gives reproducible standups.

A single router skill + per-phase reference docs + shared conventions.md is a clean, low-token pattern for a multi-step PM workflow: one always-loaded entry point, progressive disclosure into the active phase only.

For parallel AI execution, pre-compute a per-task 'stream analysis' that assigns explicit file scopes and dependency gates, then fan out one agent per scope inside a shared git worktree — gives speed without merge chaos, and the file-scope assignment is the practical 'firewall'.

Anchor an enablement program on a fixed artifact chain (problem-brief → spec → plan → tasks → build) where each step produces ONE markdown file the next consumes; the written spec, not the chat, is the durable deliverable a PM can review and version.

Ship a 'constitution' per team: a short semver-versioned principles file that every generated artifact must pass against, with conflicts flagged CRITICAL — gives leadership an anti-drift governance lever over AI output.

Adopt the 'checklist = unit test for the requirement' framing: before any build, auto-generate quality checks on the SPEC's clarity/completeness and hard-gate downstream steps on their pass rate — teaches PMs to write testable requirements.

Use a clarify step with a fixed ambiguity taxonomy that asks ≤5 targeted questions and writes answers back into the spec, before planning — institutionalizes 'reduce ambiguity early' as a tool, not a habit.

Package one prompt/template set that installs natively into whatever harness each employee uses (Claude Code skills, Copilot/Cursor markdown, Gemini TOML) via per-agent base classes — one source of truth, many runtimes, no per-team forks.
Provide org-level customization via a layered template-resolution stack + version-pinned role bundles so a central team can enforce compliant spec/plan formats without blocking local overrides.
Heed the documented forking postmortem: don't route a step to a fresh subagent for 'context savings' unless it truly returns a compact summary — measure the returned payload size first.

Encode a multi-stage PM workflow as a small set of @-referenceable markdown prompt files — no runtime, no plugin, instantly portable across Cursor/Claude Code/Windsurf/Amp. Lowest-friction way to ship an enablement 'method' to a mixed-tool org.

Make clarifying questions answerable in one line: numbered questions + lettered options ('reply 1A, 2C') dramatically lowers the cost of the intake gate and is directly reusable for any AI-PM discovery/brief skill.

Put a hard human checkpoint ('respond Go', 'approve before next sub-task') between plan and execution — a one-sentence prompt rule that turns autonomous agents into reviewable assistants without any tooling.

Use the work-artifact itself (a checkbox task file) as the progress ledger: editing `[ ]`→`[x]` after each step means a fresh session can resume from the file alone — a zero-infrastructure substitute for session-handoff memory.
Bake non-negotiable hygiene into the template (task 0.0 = create feature branch; every code file paired with a test file) so good practice is the default output, not a reviewer's afterthought.
Target outputs at a 'junior developer' reader to force explicitness — a simple, transferable forcing function for de-jargoning any spec or task list.

Make 'done' provable, not asserted: pair every AI-PM deliverable with a deterministic gate — for a PRD, a placeholder/coverage linter that floors the grade and exits non-zero; for shipped work, a re-execution oracle. The unfakable token (SHIP_CHECK_OK) is a teachable model for 'definition of done as code.'

Letter-grade rubric + reasoned-exception escape: EXCELLENT/GOOD/ACCEPTABLE/NEEDS_WORK with a HARD placeholder rule that still allows deferral IF tagged `reason:` is a clean pattern for enterprise PRD quality bars that avoids both hallucinated completeness and bureaucratic rigidity.

Stateless orchestrator + state-file pipeline: keeping routing logic out of the model and in a pipeline.json that every phase re-reads makes long multi-phase PM workflows resilient to context compaction — a directly transferable design for any multi-step agentic PM playbook.

Carry typed constraints from discovery into the spec and gate on their presence: the CONSTRAINTS CAPTURED → must-appear-in-PRD check is a concrete anti-drift mechanism worth copying for requirements traceability.

First-match single-recommendation handoff using AskUserQuestion as the durable decision record (not prose) — a good UX pattern for AI-PM tools that must hand off to humans/other tools without false 'equal options' paralysis.
Token-economy routing (complexity→cheapest-capable model, escalate only on failure, telemetry that PROVES savings) is the budget story corporate enablement programs need to justify agent fleets.

Least-privilege, return-only subagents: the research-expander's read-only toolset + 'parent does the writeback' is a safe template for fan-out research agents in a PM context (e.g. competitor/market research per task).

Make the PRD/spec the durable artifact and generate everything downstream from it: an AI-PM enablement program can ship a 'parse-spec → structured backlog' command where the written brief is parsed once into a typed, dependency-aware task list that all later steps execute against — no re-reading the brief into context.

Model the work item as a rich typed object, not a title string: include acceptance criteria, test strategy, dependencies, scope boundaries, and a free-form metadata block that survives edits. This single object then doubles as the context packet passed between any agents/people working the item.

Separate plan / execute / verify into three role agents with deliberate model tiering and tool scoping — a planner on the strong model, cheaper executors, and a read-only verifier that can only flag against the spec. The read-only constraint is what makes the review trustworthy.

Bake the methodology into the tool: complexity scoring (1-10) that auto-recommends a subtask count and a tailored breakdown template per work type (feature/bug/refactor) turns 'estimate and decompose' from tribal knowledge into a repeatable command.

Ship the same engine across every surface (CLI + MCP + plugin) from one logic core so a PM org isn't locked to one editor/agent — and so behavior is identical whether someone uses Cursor, Claude Code, or a terminal.
A small command-chaining DSL (sequence, store/recall state, conditionals, parallel+join) lets non-engineers compose multi-step PM workflows as one spec instead of running commands by hand.

Externalize ALL project state into a scaffolded folder (spec / plan / tracker / indexed memory) so AI agents are stateless executors — this is what makes 'continue tomorrow in a fresh session' actually work without re-briefing; directly transferable to an AI-PM enablement repo where PMs hand work between days/people.

Adopt the Planner→Manager→Worker split: one agent owns requirements (Spec/Plan/Rules), one owns dispatch+review, scoped workers do the doing — a clean mental model for teaching PMs how to decompose an AI-assisted initiative into roles instead of one mega-prompt.

Make Task Prompts self-contained with an enforced frontmatter contract (ids, output path, dependency flag, validation criteria) and a same-agent vs cross-agent rule for how much context to inline — a reusable template for delegating any unit of work to an AI worker without ambiguity.

Use a two-artifact handoff (a 'what happened / what's left' log + a reconstruction prompt) at context-window boundaries; teach this as the standard way to keep a long AI-PM engagement coherent across sessions and across people.

Keep command files thin and push the actual method into shared guide docs that commands cite — separating 'how to do context-gathering / work-breakdown / task-review' from 'when to run it' keeps a growing skill library readable and maintainable.

Ship one skill body that installs into every harness's skill dir (.claude/.cursor/.github/.agents/.opencode) via a CLI installer — the right packaging model for rolling an AI-PM toolkit out across a company with mixed tooling.

Spawn a fresh-context subagent that reads bulky history (archives/logs) and returns only a fixed-schema synthesis plus verification handles — gives PMs the recall benefit without polluting the main thread, and the spot-check handles guard against acting on stale context.

Make a written spec the durable artifact and compile it into project-local guardrails: an AI-PM enablement program can generate per-project 'enforcement skills/checklists' (PRD-compliance, domain-rules, compliance) that later coding sessions must load — so the PRD keeps governing implementation instead of rotting.

Use a fresh-context adversarial validator for any AI-generated claim that feeds a decision: spawn a clean-context reviewer with ONLY the claims + sources (no project memory) to break confirmation bias, with a bounded re-do loop and an explicit 'partially validated' fallback so it never hard-blocks delivery.
Thread one typed context_packet JSON through the whole workflow and hand each agent only its slice — never re-feed full conversation history; this is both a token-efficiency and a determinism lever.

Bake source-discipline into research agents with an explicit blocked_domains list + an is_official flag + a verify-the-URL step, so generated PM artifacts cite vendor/standards/gov sources rather than blogs.

Version every generated doc/skill (prd_version + Evolution Log) and add a cheap drift gate that warns when artifacts fall out of sync — making documentation staleness a detectable, actionable signal rather than silent rot.

Ship a delta-aware 'evolve' command: when scope changes, classify the change, compute affected artifacts, and regenerate ONLY those in parallel while reporting what was preserved — cheaper and safer than full regeneration.

One-question-at-a-time structured interrogation across fixed blocks (with explicit 'skip -> record Known Gaps') is a reusable intake pattern for any PM tool that must elicit messy requirements before generating.

Encode the deliverable as a numbered, machine-checkable output contract (here 64 rules across structure/traceability/security/testing) plus a mandatory pre-delivery self-check that fixes violations — this is the single biggest lever for getting consistent, audit-ready PM artifacts out of an LLM.

Adopt an honest verdict taxonomy and explicitly ban universal-PASS: forcing PASS/SPEC-COMPLETE/NEEDS-RUNTIME/INCONCLUSIVE/FAIL labels on claims is a cheap, powerful anti-confirmation-bias mechanism any enablement program can lift.

Gate generation behind a real elicitation loop: ask structured clarification questions (via a structured-question tool, fixed option/header limits) and refuse to generate until the user explicitly says proceed — plus a scope/feasibility gate that negotiates down oversized asks before any work.

Scale rigor to artifact type with a small static table (sections / questions / research-depth / strategy per document type) rather than treating every doc the same — a proposal and an incident postmortem should not get identical depth.

Tier verification by cost: run free deterministic structural checks first (graph cycles, broken traceability, story-point math), then spend LLM/judge calls only on the claims that survive — and instrument which reasoning strategies actually move confidence so the method improves over time.

Make the codebase itself first-class context: an /index-codebase step that extracts architecture patterns + entities + dependencies into a hybrid BM25+vector store, then forces every technical requirement to trace to a real code finding, prevents architecture-blind PRDs.

Make the PM workflow a versioned artifact contract: define stages (problem-brief -> PRD -> design -> task-breakdown) once in a YAML/JSON schema with explicit requires[] gates, and let one engine enforce ordering — so every PM produces the same shaped artifacts and a downstream step can't start before its inputs exist.

Folder-per-initiative with a propose/apply/archive lifecycle is a clean mental model for AI-PM work: an active change folder holds the living proposal+spec+tasks, and archiving reconciles deltas into a durable source-of-truth spec tree — gives provenance and a tidy 'current state vs in-flight' separation.

Separate the durable spec store from in-flight deltas: never let an agent overwrite canonical specs; have it emit ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED deltas that are reviewed and merged on completion. This is how you keep an LLM-maintained KB trustworthy.

Push rules to a CLI/tool the agent calls just-in-time rather than a bloated system prompt — give the agent `get-instructions <stage>` and `get-status` commands so it pulls only the current step's template + rules, keeping context small and the workflow enforceable in code.

Author one workflow and fan it out to every harness your org uses via an adapter layer + auto-detection, plus an `init`/`update`/`validate` CLI, so the same PM process installs identically into Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, etc. and a linter gates output format.
Add an explicit 'explore' (thinking-partner) command before 'propose' to legitimize the divergent/diagnosis phase as a distinct, non-committal step in the AI-PM ritual.

Extract context from artifacts you already have rather than asking PMs to author docs from scratch: a 'discover-standards'-style pass that reads existing specs/PRDs/decks and proposes documented conventions (asking 'why' per pattern) is a high-yield onboarding move for an AI-PM enablement program.

Use a lightweight YAML/markdown index as the retrieval layer before reaching for a vector DB — a `index.yml` of {category: {doc: one-line-description}} plus a 'read index, then load only the 2-5 relevant docs' rule gives progressive disclosure with zero infra.

Make planning artifacts self-contained and discoverable: a fixed spec-folder shape (decisions / plan / applicable-standards / reference-examples / visuals) with a 'save the spec before doing any work' gate keeps AI work auditable and resumable.

Separate user-invoked /commands from auto-fired skills, and have an injection command that adapts its OUTPUT format to the scenario (inline vs embed-in-skill vs embed-in-spec) — the same knowledge served three ways by context.

Right-size the framework to the model's current weaknesses: agent-os v3's choice to retire its own orchestration/subagents because frontier models now do that natively is a useful enablement-program lesson — invest enablement effort where tools are still weak (org-specific standards, spec quality), not where they're already strong.
Treat token economy as an authoring standard ('lead with the rule, code over prose, skip the obvious') so injected context stays cheap and scannable.

Ship ONE loop-engineering reference doc and have every iterative skill cite it: standardizing Gather-Act-Observe-Verify-Update-Decide + layered termination + 'never trust the agent's self-report' makes agent reliability a reusable contract, not per-skill folklore — directly transferable to a corporate AI-PM enablement playbook.

Adopt the Worker Output Rule verbatim: a <2K-inline / >=2K-to-file threshold with status+path returns is the cheapest possible context-firewall and keeps an expensive reasoning model's window clean during multi-step PM workflows.

Model-tier the team: cheap model for collection/I/O/publishing/pre-approved mutations, frontier model for synthesis/judgment, encoded as an ALWAYS-APPLY routing table in the root instruction file — a concrete cost lever for any enablement program running at scale.

Make citations mechanical gates, not etiquette: '[Source: [[note]] | date | confidence]' on every durable claim, and drop any synthesized principle lacking >=1 source link — an anti-hallucination control that's auditable and teachable.

Tier people/entity profiles by counted evidence (stub->moderate->full) and keep them append-only + source-cited so they stay shareable; gives PMs a stakeholder/CRM memory that grows honestly instead of speculating.

Package the same skill surface across runtimes and police it with a CI validator (count parity, every skill documented in the universal AGENTS.md fallback, version alignment) — the model for distributing a PM-OS to a mixed-tool org without surface drift.

Drive every skill off two tiny config files (MY-PROFILE.md role/projects/agent_mode + MY-INTEGRATIONS.md active/disabled tools) so behavior personalizes and degrades gracefully (solo vs team) per user with zero code changes.

Separate compiled_truth (synthesized, cited assessment) from a raw dated timeline on every entity page, and enforce a source-precedence ladder (user statements > compiled truth > timeline > external) — a clean, teachable pattern for PM knowledge pages (accounts, features, competitors) where 'what we currently believe' must be distinguishable from 'the raw evidence'.

Make the query layer RETURN A CITED ANSWER WITH EXPLICIT GAP FLAGS, not a list of documents: a contract that bans hallucination, requires a slug-traceable [Source: ...] on every claim, and says 'the brain doesn't have information on X' rather than guessing — directly portable to a corporate PM Q&A assistant.

Routing as data, not a maintained table: discover skills at runtime by parsing YAML 'triggers:' frontmatter and ship a per-skill routing-eval.jsonl that CI-tests dispatch accuracy. An AI-PM enablement program can grow its skill library without an ever-growing hand-edited router.

Self-wiring typed-edge graph with zero-LLM extraction: parse [Name](people/slug) links and infer relationship types deterministically on write, then expose graph-query for 'who works at / who invested in / who attended'. Cheap, auditable relationship memory for stakeholder/account graphs without an LLM-extraction bill.

Treat skill files as improvable artifacts: a validation-gated, budget-capped optimizer (median-of-3 + held-out set) that rewrites only the skill body and quarantines bundled skills behind explicit flags — a governance model for letting an enablement team safely auto-improve prompts.

An always-on ambient capture subagent (fires every message, never blocks the reply, captures verbatim original thinking + entities) plus a nightly consolidation cycle that filters with a cheap model and synthesizes with a frontier model — the capture+consolidation loop a PM 'second brain' needs to stay current without manual filing.

Durable, observable, steerable background work as a Postgres queue with token accounting and mid-flight steering, gated by a 'pain-triggered' policy (native subagent first, durable queue only when restarts/parallelism/long-runtime/state-drop actually hurt) — a pragmatic default that avoids over-engineering orchestration.

Keep the root instruction file as orientation + a load-on-demand reference map, and ENFORCE the size cap in CI — gbrain explicitly documents that its own CLAUDE.md ballooned to 147k tokens as an append-only index and fixed it with a guard. The cautionary tale plus the fix is the whole CM-01/progressive-disclosure lesson in one repo.

Model a PM 'team' as a roster of named-role slash commands (discovery PM, strategy lead, design critic, QA, security) wired into a fixed lifecycle, rather than one mega-prompt — the sprint chain (Think->Plan->Build->Review->Test->Ship->Reflect) is the enablement product.

Use a filesystem-convention handoff: each skill writes a timestamped artifact to a per-project folder and the next skill auto-discovers the latest by glob+timestamp — eliminates fragile path-passing and makes the doc the source of truth (directly portable to PRD->design->build->review chains).

Adopt a tiered always-loaded preamble: declare a tier per skill and compose cumulative shared context (voice, output-completeness contract, confusion protocol, capture loop) so every skill inherits org standards without duplicating them in each prompt.

Encode self-improvement cheaply: append-only JSONL learnings/decisions per project, dedup by (key,type) latest-wins, decay confidence at read-time, and surface the top-N at skill start — a lightweight 'org memory' a PM team can run without a vector DB.

Gate quality with a fresh-context reviewer subagent that scores the artifact on fixed dimensions and re-dispatches until PASS (max N), logging unresolved 'Reviewer Concerns' into the doc — a placeholder-proof, anti-confirmation-bias contract for PRDs and decision memos.

Provide an unattended 'auto-decide principle set' so a workflow can run end-to-end while still escalating only genuine 'user-challenge' decisions to a human gate — lets enablement scale from hand-held to autonomous.

Make the spec the durable artifact and run each stage in a fresh-context subagent that reads the prior doc and writes the next (RESEARCH→PLAN→SUMMARY→VERIFICATION); the orchestrator only ever sees compact status blocks, which keeps long programs from drowning the main context.

Encode an adversarial verifier as a distinct fresh agent with a falsify-first stance and a concrete evidence ladder (exists→substantive→wired→data-flows) so 'done' means observable behavior, not checked boxes — directly transferable to reviewing PM deliverables (PRD claims, dashboards, GTM artifacts) for hollowness.

Keep team knowledge in one greppable predicate doc where each fact is an addressable ID and briefs must cite IDs verbatim — gives an AI-PM org an anti-drift, audit-traceable single source of truth that both humans and agents load fresh before any task.

Split user-invoked /commands (with explicit argument-hint flag grammar and a requires: dependency list) from spawned agents (markdown+frontmatter with least-privilege tool allowlists) — a clean mental model for an enablement program: commands are the menu, agents are the workers.

Tag every research claim with provenance/confidence ([VERIFIED]/[CITED]/[ASSUMED]) and force anything unverified into a human-validation gate before decisions lock — a lightweight anti-hallucination discipline any PM research workflow can adopt.

Make context budget a first-class, agent-visible signal (inject warnings into the agent stream, snapshot state before exhaustion) rather than a silent failure — useful for long unattended PM automations.

Use a shared, addressed memory namespace (a blackboard like swarm/<role>/status, swarm/shared/<decision>) as the team's coordination substrate so PM sub-agents publish status/decisions to predictable keys and read peers before acting — decoupled and auditable, no brittle hand-offs.

Wire Claude Code lifecycle hooks (SessionStart import, Stop/SessionEnd sync, PreCompact restore-then-persist, SubagentStop capture) to make memory survive compaction and session boundaries automatically — the single biggest reliability win for long PM workstreams.

Define agents as plain markdown + minimal YAML frontmatter (name + a what/when description) with a Responsibilities / Approach / Collaboration body that names sibling agents — cheap to author, easy to review, version-controllable.

Adopt the 3-level progressive-disclosure budget for skills (tiny always-on metadata, SKILL.md on trigger, reference files on demand) so a large skill library doesn't bloat context — and ship a skill-builder meta-skill so the team can generate compliant new skills.

Make the written spec/ADR the binding source of truth with an explicit precedence rule (ADR governs architecture, SPEC governs scope) baked into each doer agent's prompt — a concrete anti-drift gate for AI-PM teams.
Promote review/consensus to named agents (a critic/Byzantine-style verifier, weighted voting) rather than ad hoc prompts when multiple agents must agree on a decision.

Define an always-loaded ~120-token CRITICAL_FACTS core (role, manager, org, timezone, location) plus a progressive L0-L3 context loader so every PM session bootstraps the same shared identity without re-explaining; cap each tier with an explicit token budget.

Make notes machine-first: mandate a 'For future Claude' 2-3 sentence preamble + structured frontmatter on every artifact, and enforce it with a cheap lint hook (grep for the preamble, required fields, banned unicode) rather than trusting the model to comply.

Adopt a two-output rule + propagation matrix: every answer also writes back to the knowledge base and must cascade to 3-5 related pages, so a PM team's wiki self-maintains and never accumulates orphan notes.

Use bi-temporal facts (a timeline array recording valid-time and ingest-time) for decisions and competitive intel so the org can answer 'what did we believe, and when did it change' — invaluable for post-mortems and strategy drift.
Set a rewrite-depth quality bar for ingestion: judge a research/ingest task as incomplete if it only appended new pages and rewrote nothing — this forces synthesis over note-hoarding.
Ship one skill source that compiles to multiple CLIs via an adapters/+build.sh layer, so a corporate enablement pack isn't locked to a single vendor (Claude Code / Codex / Gemini / OpenCode).

Run scheduled background agents (nightly reconcile/synthesize/heal-orphans, weekly review, health audit) so the knowledge base improves unattended — the maintenance burden is automated, not assigned to a person.
For decisions, use a disagreement-preserving advisor panel that surfaces the split instead of papering over it — more honest input to PM trade-off calls than a forced-consensus committee.

Ship a memory-layer template set (SOUL/USER/MEMORY/daily) as the starting scaffold for any AI-PM agent so identity, profile, and decision-history persist across sessions without re-prompting — and load them via a SessionStart hook, not manual paste.

Use PreCompact + SessionEnd hooks that spawn a zero-tool 'flush' agent to distill each session into an append-only log — the durable, cheap way to give an enablement-program agent real continuity instead of relying on the context window.

Adopt the two-phase proactive loop (deterministic Python gather → LLM reason → state-diff → notify only on change) as the template for any 'always-watching' PM assistant; it makes proactivity affordable and non-spammy, with a concrete cost delta to cite.

Standardize integrations as Python CLI wrappers (query.py <platform> <subcommand>) so the LLM only ever sees structured data and never credentials — pair with a PreToolUse block-secrets hook as the security story for a corporate rollout.

Encode a calibrated autonomy menu (Observer/Advisor/Assistant/Partner) in the requirements/elicitation step so each user explicitly sets how bold the agent may be — a clean governance pattern for enterprise AI-PM deployments.
Make onboarding a self-deleting BOOTSTRAP wizard that interviews once and writes the config files — lowers setup friction for non-technical PMs while keeping each agent personalized.

Treat the PRD itself as the deliverable: a skill that interviews + researches APIs + emits a phased, personalized build plan is a strong template for 'AI-PM jumpstart' programs where the output is an executable spec, not code.

Give the agent a fixed bootstrap read-order (long-term memory -> recent dailies -> current-period goals -> handoff) so every session reconstructs context deterministically instead of re-deriving it — a cheap, copyable continuity pattern for any AI-PM workspace.

Model memory with explicit decay + tiers, not an ever-growing log: a frontmatter 'tier' field + per-type decay rates + 'touch promotes one tier' keeps a knowledge base self-pruning so stale items fade and active ones stay hot — directly transferable to a PM's accounts/initiatives/notes.

Split deterministic metrics from LLM judgment: let a script compute a 0-100 'knowledge-base health score' (broken links, orphans, description coverage) and gate agent actions on thresholds — gives a corporate program objective, auditable KB-quality SLAs.

Enforce output contracts hard ('raw HTML only, 6-tag allowlist, <=4096 chars, idempotent if already-processed marker present') — a clean template for making agent outputs safe to pipe into downstream channels (Slack/Teams/Telegram) at scale.

Run a cheap-model->frontier-model two-wave swarm (haiku triage in ~50K-token batches -> sonnet validation via map-reduce) to classify a large unstructured corpus affordably — a reusable bootstrap recipe for onboarding a backlog of legacy docs.

Use an isolated, fully self-contained scheduled-session pattern for autonomous recurring work, kept separate from the interactive session — the model for 'always-on' PM routines (digest, inbox triage) that must not leak or depend on live chat state.

A flat markdown brain/ (business context, active projects, recent decisions, identity, constitution) indexed by SQLite FTS5 gives an AI-PM enablement program durable, searchable team memory with no vector-DB overhead — and a one-file constitution.md is a cheap, powerful anti-drift gate.

Make every skill a folder with SKILL.md + eval.json + learnings.md so skills carry their own success criteria and accumulated lessons; a weekly consolidation job that dedups learnings and prunes score-history turns a skill library into a self-improving asset rather than static prompts.

Declare Primary+Fallback runtime per skill: this is how you let one skill catalog run across multiple model tiers (cheap-for-triage, frontier-for-reasoning) and survive any single provider outage — directly transferable to enterprises hedging across vendors.

Drop-in JSON cron files + a file-watcher hot-reload pattern lets non-engineers schedule recurring agent routines (daily standup, weekly memory consolidation, cost audits) by adding a file — a low-friction automation surface for a PM team.

Operationalize governance: turn each principle into a concrete mechanism (Transparency -> audit log + git auto-commit; Cost Consciousness -> 80/90/95% spend alerts; Resilience -> per-skill fallback). Principles that map to running code actually constrain behavior.

Make 'one agent owns the raw input, everyone else reads its summary' a default pattern — a corporate AI-PM KB should never let a 50-page PRD or a long call transcript leak into every downstream agent; designate a single ingest-writer and a fixed summary page contract.

Adopt INDEX-based progressive disclosure (index.yaml summary → page → highlights → raw) as the cheap-but-good alternative to standing up a vector DB for an internal product-knowledge wiki — most PM Q&A is answerable at the index/summary layer.

Split the pipeline into LLM-for-synthesis and deterministic-Python-for-bookkeeping (index build, dedup, merge, timestamps) so the KB is idempotent and byte-stable across re-runs — critical for a shared, version-controlled team artifact.

Ship a lint/health skill alongside the writer skill: mechanical graph checks (orphans, missing hub pages above a mention threshold, broken links) + LLM checks (stale/contradictory claims) keep an LLM-maintained KB from rotting as it grows.

Gate KB structure on corroboration: only promote a concept/person/tool to its own page when it's mentioned across >=2 distinct sources — a simple, transferable rule that keeps a product-knowledge graph signal-dense.

Use an explicit between-rounds gap-analyzer that grades coverage and writes the next queries from metadata only — a clean template for any iterative competitive/market-research workflow where you want depth presets (fast/light/deep) the PM can dial.
Preserve human edits in machine-rewritten docs via <!-- KEEP --> markers and byte-identical noop skips — essential for trust when agents continuously rewrite shared wiki pages.

Ship PM frameworks as executable, stdlib-only CLIs (RICE, ICE, opportunity scoring) the agent shells out to — deterministic math beats asking the LLM to 'compute' a score, and the tool runs offline with --sample self-tests; mandate a --help/--sample smoke test as the acceptance gate.

Adopt a one-page binding SKILL.md contract (Before-Starting context checkpoint, Proactive Triggers, Output-Artifacts table, Related-Skills WHEN/NOT disambiguation, 🟢/🟡/🔴 confidence tagging) and enforce it with a CI checklist runner — this is how you keep a corporate skill library consistent across many authors.

Bake session continuity into hooks, not habits: a SessionStart hook that injects a structured 5-section handoff (Goal/State/Decisions/Skills/Artifacts) and a SessionEnd nudge if none was written — with a 'reference by path, never duplicate' rule to keep handoffs token-cheap.

Use a transient→permanent memory promotion ladder: cheap MEMORY.md auto-capture, then human-approved graduation to the governing CLAUDE.md only after a pattern recurs, deleting the transient entry — gives a self-improving program without unbounded context growth or unvetted rules.

Pair every skill with a named persona agent (least-privilege tool allowlist, model:inherit) and a slash command, and keep an explicit commands-vs-skills split so PMs get both auto-invocation and a discoverable manual surface.

Enforce 'no silent chaining': orchestrators ask one recommended-answer question before routing below a confidence threshold — a safer default than autonomous multi-agent fan-out for enterprise rollout.

A megaprompt→skill conversion pipeline lets you industrialize: capture an expert's prompt, drop it in verbatim as SKILL.md, and auto-wrap validators/references/agent/command/provenance — the fastest path from tribal knowledge to a governed skill.

Give every AI-PM agent a fixed 'context-request' template it must fill before producing output — a JSON or bullet block enumerating the exact facts that role needs (vision, target users, metrics, success criteria). It doubles as a checklist for the human PM of what context to supply and prevents the agent from inventing missing facts.

Adopt one rigid agent-anatomy across the whole enablement library: identity → invocation steps → fixed-length domain checklists → context protocol → 3-phase workflow → named collaborators. Uniformity makes agents skimmable, reviewable, and lint-able, and lowers the authoring cost of adding the next role.

Encode model + tool allowlist per persona in frontmatter (cheap model + read/write/web only for doc-generation roles; stronger model for analytical roles) — bakes cost control and least-privilege into each agent rather than leaving it to runtime config.
Ship a collaborator list inside each agent (which sibling roles it hands off to) so a multi-role PM team self-documents its workflow graph without a central orchestrator.
Package and distribute role packs as installable plugins per category, with a self-service installer agent, so a corporate program can roll out 'the product team' as one bundle.

Treat agent/skill quality as MEASURED, not asserted: stand up a plugin-eval-style harness (cheap static lint → LLM-judge on a few semantic dimensions → a handful of real simulated invocations) and gate every new PM agent/skill on a composite score before it ships to the org.

Make 'triggering accuracy' the first thing you optimize — the rubric weights it 0.25 (highest) and the methodology skill states it gives the fastest composite gains; for an AI-PM enablement program that means investing in crisp 'Use this when...' descriptions over fancy bodies.

Author once, deliver everywhere: keep a single markdown source of truth for each agent/skill and generate harness-native artifacts, so the PM org isn't maintaining divergent copies for Claude Code vs Cursor vs Copilot.
Encode anti-patterns as automatic penalties (over-constraining language, empty descriptions, missing triggers, dead cross-refs) so quality drift is caught mechanically rather than in review.

Use the team-lead/worker pattern for multi-step PM work: an opus orchestrator decomposes into file-ownership-bounded parallel tasks, workers return clean summaries (context firewall), lead synthesizes — with model-tiering (cheaper models for narrow workers) to control cost.
Run a 'doc gardener' drift check (dead links, stale files, oversized bodies) on the whole knowledge/skill corpus in CI to keep a large catalog coherent as it grows.

Ship a hooks.json starter for the PM team: SessionStart loads the relevant area README + open decisions, SessionEnd appends a learning-log entry — making 'remember across sessions' a harness guarantee, not a habit.

Adopt the context-manager's explicit handoff briefing contract (objective + file paths + constraints + expected output; exclude debug history/rejected approaches) as the standard template every PM subagent must emit when delegating.
Borrow the five named orchestration topologies as a shared vocabulary so PMs can say 'run this as Map-Reduce' or 'Debate-with-judge' instead of ad-hoc prompting.

Define PM agents as frontmatter files with a tools allowlist + model + a HARD output contract (e.g. ≥3 acceptance criteria/story, baselined metrics, rollback criteria) so PRD quality is enforced, not hoped for.
Treat skills like packages: a 'npx skills add owner/repo' install path plus a thin curated allowlist lets a corporate program centrally bless community skills while preserving provenance frontmatter.

Note the gap as a lesson: this catalog has rich engineering skills but ZERO PM/product skill and no PM command — PM capability sits only in agent personas. A jumpstart program should fill the missing PM skill+command layer (RICE calculator, PRD-evolve, discovery interview).

Anchor an AI-PM enablement pack on ONE shared, agent-authored product-context file (positioning/ICP/JTBD/competitors/proof) that every skill reads first — this single move kills the repeated 'tell me about your product' tax and is the highest-leverage pattern in the repo.
Ship skills with packed trigger-phrase descriptions AND explicit scope cross-refs ('For X, see Y') — auto-invocation and clean handoffs both depend on the description field, not the body.

Make skill quality a CI gate: a cheap bash linter (name=dir, description length, trigger-word presence, <500 lines, version-under-metadata) plus an official validator, fanned out per changed skill, keeps a large library consistent as contributors grow.
Auto-generate the catalog/index (marketplace + README table) from the skills folder via CI so documentation never drifts — critical once you pass ~20 skills.

Bundle a zero-dependency executable tool layer with a universal --dry-run so skills can actually touch GA4/Stripe/CRM safely, and keep an index/registry file as the map agents read before loading any one integration guide (just-in-time, not all-at-once).

Encode named frameworks (JTBD Four Forces, impact-ordered CRO diagnostic, ORB channels) directly into the prompt body with worked structure, so the skill teaches method rather than just generating a doc — and split diagnosis skills (analyze) from generation skills (produce).
Keep skills harness-portable by routing one runtime's power features into a separate AGENTS.md/overrides layer, never into the shared skill body.

For multi-aspect evaluation work (vendor reviews, design reviews, launch readiness), shard by EVALUATION DIMENSION not by task: one subagent per scoring axis with an explicit per-band rubric, launched in parallel, then reduced by a published weight vector — the weights make the org's priorities legible and reviewable.

Make subagent prompts end with a rigid return-block template so only a compact structured summary re-enters the orchestrator context; this is how you keep a 5-way fan-out from blowing the main window with raw fetch dumps.

Run a cheap shared 'discovery/classification' pass once and inject the result (here: business-type + page-map) into every parallel worker, so they share framing instead of each re-deriving it 5 times.

Coordinate multi-step deliverables through named artifact files in a working folder and have downstream steps opportunistically fold in whichever exist — a no-infra handoff bus that also gives the user inspectable intermediate outputs.

Split judgment from rendering for client deliverables: constrain the model to emit a strict JSON schema, then let deterministic code (reportlab/charts) render the polished PDF — reproducible formatting, no LLM layout drift.

Ship as a flat installer (install.sh copies into ~/.claude/skills and ~/.claude/agents) with a single namespaced command surface (/market <verb>) — low-friction distribution pattern for an internal enablement pack.

Decompose a broad function (here: SEO) into one router skill + many single-responsibility sub-skills + tool-scoped specialist agents + deterministic scripts; the 3-layer directive/orchestration/execution split keeps each piece testable and the router thin.

Put domain knowledge in references/ files loaded on demand, not in the SKILL.md body — the orchestrator only needs a routing table, so context stays cheap until a specialist actually needs detail (progressive disclosure).

Gate subagent spawning on runtime signals (detected context, available integrations, prior state) so a workflow scales its team to the actual task instead of always running the full roster — directly transferable to PM audits that vary by product type.

Make the inter-step contract a typed JSON artifact (audit-data.json) that a separate rendering step consumes — decouples reasoning from deliverable production and lets you regenerate reports without re-running analysis.
Enforce output quality with a PostToolUse hook that validates every file the agent writes (schema validation here) — an inline CI gate rather than trusting the model's output.

Design for portability and graceful degradation: write delegation so it runs as parallel subagents where supported and falls back to sequential inline where not, and expose the same assets to multiple harnesses via plugin.json + AGENTS.md.
Ground every recommendation in a falsifiable observation + success metric + failure-mode check — a reusable rubric for keeping AI-PM outputs evidence-bound rather than generic.

A 'constitution' file of 5-10 non-negotiable principles, compiled into explicit Mandatory-vs-Advisory check gates that a planning step must pass (and that BLOCK execution when unjustified), is a clean anti-drift mechanism an AI-PM program can adopt for any artifact type — bind the gates to your own quality bar (e.g. 'evidence-bound, named owner, measurable success metric').

Spec→plan→tasks→implement as a document-handoff chain — each step writes one artifact the next reads, with a small bootstrap script emitting a JSON contract (paths + branch) — is a runtime-agnostic way to make multi-step PM work repeatable and auditable without any memory/DB layer.

Encode the SUCCESS METRICS into the methodology up front and make them a quantitative go/no-go checkpoint (here: pilot k-factor/engagement thresholds that pause execution) — forces measurement-first thinking and prevents 'ship anyway' drift; a corporate PM jumpstart can wire RICE/NSM thresholds the same way.

A self-validating spec: after generating, the agent writes its own quality checklist, scores the spec against it, and iterates up to N times until green — a cheap, harness-independent output-quality contract worth copying into any doc-generation skill.

Multi-kit namespace isolation (own hidden dir, own command prefix, own env var, own package) is a practical pattern for shipping several role-specific 'kits' (PM, growth, design, blog) that coexist in one repo/agent without command collisions — directly relevant to a SoftServe enablement program serving multiple job functions.

One source dir + a CI matrix that fans out to N agent × M script variants with automated validation (no leftover upstream refs, correct structure, correct governance version) lets a single team maintain cross-harness skills (Claude Code/Cursor/Gemini/Copilot) from one place.

Standardize on the canonical SKILL.md: folder-named, two-required-field frontmatter (name + description), with ALL triggering intelligence packed into a deliberately pushy, keyword-dense, exclusion-bearing description — this single convention makes skills discoverable and is the cheapest enablement win for an AI-PM program.

Teach the three-tier loading budget explicitly (always-on metadata / on-trigger body <500 lines / JIT references + executable scripts) so PMs design skills that scale without blowing the context window — pointer-to-reference-file with a TOC beats inlining.

Adopt skill-creator as the enablement flywheel: a meta-skill + template that interviews the PM, drafts the skill, auto-runs with-skill vs baseline evals, and produces a quantitative benchmark — turning 'write a prompt' into a measured, iterated artifact non-engineers can run.

Use the description-optimization loop (20 trigger/non-trigger queries, train/held-out split, x3 runs) as the QA gate for any skill library — it catches the silent failure mode of skills that never fire or fire on the wrong request.

Borrow the grader's anti-surface-compliance stance (PASS only on genuine task completion, burden-of-proof on the assertion, plus critique the evals themselves) as the rubric for reviewing any PM deliverable produced by an agent.

Ship subagent roles as plain markdown Role/Inputs/Process/Output-Format docs in an agents/ folder, loaded only when spawned — a no-infrastructure way to give a PM team reusable reviewer/grader/analyzer personas.

Package skills as an installable plugin via a .claude-plugin/marketplace.json manifest grouping skills into named bundles (document-skills, example-skills) so a whole capability pack installs with one /plugin install command.

Standardize one plugin layout for the whole org: .claude-plugin/plugin.json + skills/<name>/SKILL.md + agents/<name>.md + commands/ + .mcp.json, and ship a single 'example-plugin' as the canonical reference everyone copies — this is exactly how Anthropic bootstraps contributors and would let an AI-PM enablement team package each PM job-function as an installable bundle.

Treat skills as evaluated artifacts, not prose: borrow skill-creator's loop (interview -> draft SKILL.md -> generate quantitative evals -> background test runs -> description-improver to fix under-triggering). For a PM program, every framework skill (RICE, JTBD, Mom Test) should ship with test prompts and a 'pushy' trigger description.

Orchestrate multi-agent workflows from a single command markdown file with explicit model-tiering — cheap models (Haiku) for triage/scoring, frontier models (Sonnet) for the actual review — and fan out N parallel specialists on different facets. A PM 'PRD review' or 'launch readiness' command could fan out reviewers (legal/eng-feasibility/GTM/exec-skeptic) the same way.

Gate findings with a per-item confidence judge and a numeric threshold so noisy parallel output gets filtered to high-precision results before it reaches the human — directly transferable to PM artifact QA (e.g. flagging only high-confidence gaps in a spec).

Make the instruction doc a living, audited asset: pair an auto-invoked 'audit my CLAUDE.md against reality' skill with an end-of-session '/revise-claude-md capture learnings' command, so project memory self-maintains — the same split (periodic audit + capture loop) maps to a PM team's living product-context / decision-log doc.

Curate, don't author everything: a vendor-owned marketplace.json can expose a curated SUBSET of skills (strict:false skills[]) out of partner/SDK repos that never adopted your format — a corporate AI-PM program can similarly register the good bits of external skill libraries without forking them.
